description: Figure 1 presents schematic diagrams of two experimental setups employed for cyclic testing of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. Subfigure (a) illustrates the thermocycling test apparatus, featuring restriction supports, a centrally mounted thermocouple for temperature monitoring, and a cylindrical specimen with a 5 mm diameter (φ=5mm) working gauge section. The electrical circuit includes an AC battery and an automatic temperature-controlled variable resistor for resistive heating, enabling cyclic thermal loading between 873 K and 313 K under conditions of 80% thermal expansion restriction. Subfigure (b) depicts the elasto-plastic bending test machine (ZDM-200PU), showing a larger cylindrical specimen with 50 mm diameter (φ=50mm) secured between a fixed head and a moving table, with a mechanical handle for applying constant deflection. The setup maintains a free-end deflection of 150 mm during alternating bending tests. These complementary systems were used to evaluate material response under combined thermal cycling and elasto-plastic mechanical deformation.

Figure 2 presents S-N curves (stress amplitude versus number of cycles to failure) for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under three distinct testing conditions: (1) room temperature (293 K, diamonds), (2) elevated temperature (673 K, circles), and (3) thermal cycling between 293 K and 673 K (triangles). The y-axis displays applied stress amplitude in MPa (220–540 MPa range) and the x-axis shows number of cycles N on a logarithmic scale (0.1–50 × 10⁶). Curve 2 (673 K) exhibits higher fatigue strength than Curve 1 (293 K), indicating anomalous temperature dependence where elevated temperature improves fatigue resistance. The thermocycling Curve 3 occupies an intermediate position between the isothermal tests. Horizontal arrows denote run-out specimens that did not fail within the tested cycle limits. This data demonstrates that Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys exhibit superior fatigue resistance at 673 K compared to room temperature, supporting the conclusion that time-dependent deformation aging in microplastic deformation zones enhances high-temperature fatigue performance, and that components can successfully withstand cyclic elasto-plastic loading under periodic temperature changes.

Figure 3 presents a comparative optical microscopy analysis (30 μm scale bar) of microstructural evolution in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel versus martensitic Cr-Ni steel under cyclic testing conditions. Subfigure (a) shows the as-received austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel characterized by equiaxed austenite grains with discrete κ-phase precipitates decorating grain boundaries. Subfigure (b) reveals the same alloy after thermal cycling, where caption states the grain boundary κ-phase precipitates have undergone significant thickening/coarsening during high-temperature aging, consistent with thermal fatigue exposure between 293 K and 673 K. Subfigures (c) and (d) display the martensitic Cr-Ni steel before and after testing respectively; (c) exhibits the characteristic lath martensite structure with distinct crystallographic orientation, while (d) shows complete decomposition of the martensitic solid solution into an equilibrium ferrite-carbide mixture with loss of the original martensitic orientation. These micrographs visually demonstrate the contrasting thermal stability mechanisms: precipitation coarsening in the austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C system versus martensite tempering/decomposition in the Cr-Ni steel, supporting the paper's conclusion regarding microstructural degradation pathways under cyclic elasto-plastic deformation and thermal loading.

This figure presents force-deflection hysteresis loops for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel (specifically Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.86C-0.7W-0.43Mo-0.49Nb) subjected to alternating elasto-plastic bending fatigue testing. The graph plots applied load Q (in units of ×10³ N, ranging from -50 to +50) against deflection H (in mm, ranging from -200 to +200) for a specimen with 50 mm diameter. Three distinct curves are labeled: the 1st Cycle (dotted line), 2nd Cycle (dashed line), and Last Cycle (12th) (solid line), illustrating the evolution of the hysteresis loop with increasing cycle number. The figure demonstrates that the hysteresis loop stabilizes rapidly, with minimal variation in the work spent on elasto-plastic deformation from cycle to cycle, as evidenced by the nearly overlapping loops of the 2nd and 12th cycles. This mechanical stability under cyclic loading supports the conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys can sustain significantly more cycles (approximately four times) compared to martensitic chromium steels under identical service conditions, validating their suitability for components subjected to periodic elasto-plastic deformation.

This figure presents the true stress–true strain curve of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel, illustrating its strain hardening behavior under monotonic tensile loading at a strain rate of 1 mm/min. The curve demonstrates continuous work hardening from the origin up to approximately 1400 MPa at a true strain of 0.4. Key data points visible on the curve indicate true stress values of roughly 500 MPa at 0.05 strain, 800 MPa at 0.1 strain, 1000 MPa at 0.2 strain, and 1200 MPa at 0.25 strain, showing a transition from rapid initial hardening to a nearly linear hardening regime at higher strains. As stated in the caption and supported by the citation text, this exceptional strain hardening capacity is attributed to the austenitic structure's deformation mechanism involving mechanical twinning, where deformation twins act as barriers to dislocation motion and promote localized dislocation tangles. The curve provides fundamental mechanical property data for understanding the alloy's high endurance under elasto-plastic cyclic loading conditions described in the study of Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.86C steel.

This figure presents a pair of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrating the evolution of dislocation structures in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel (specifically Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.86C with minor W, Mo, Nb additions) subjected to cold working at different deformation levels. Subfigure (a) shows the dislocation configuration after 10% cold working, while subfigure (b) displays the structure after 20% cold working, both captured at the same magnification with a scale bar of 625 nm. The images reveal that with increasing plastic deformation, the dislocation density increases sharply and localized dislocation tangles form at intersections of slip systems. These high-density dislocation arrays and tangles act as obstacles to further plastic deformation, contributing to the alloy's exceptional strengthening capacity. The caption and citation context indicate that these microstructural features explain the high endurance of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys under elasto-plastic cyclic loading conditions, where continuously forming highly strengthened local volumes maintain invariant absorbed energy per cycle until failure occurs throughout the entire working volume.
